contrastMetadata.transferBoundary: Pay preprocessing cost only when the query volume, mutation model, memory budget, and preserved semantics make the total workload cheaper than repeated scans.

items.prompt: A static unsorted array will receive one million exact-membership queries. Reordering is allowed and all queries use the same comparison key. Which strategy is usually the stronger asymptotic choice?

options.text: Sort once, then binary-search each query.
Scan the full unsorted array for every query.
Binary-search the unsorted array without preprocessing.
Sort the array again before every query.

feedback.reason: Repeated queries can amortize a one-time ordering cost.
feedback.details: The full workload is O(n log n + q log n) instead of O(qn), provided sorting preserves the required semantics.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_each: This repeats O(n) work one million times.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_unsorted: The midpoint comparison has no legal direction.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_each_query: The static data needs preprocessing only once.

items.prompt: The same unsorted array will receive only one exact query. Which default asymptotic strategy is preferable when no sorted representation already exists?

options.text: Scan once in O(n).
Sort in O(n log n) and then binary-search.
Binary-search directly.
Build a sorted copy and discard it immediately after the query.

feedback.reason: The query count changes whether preprocessing can pay for itself.
feedback.details: A one-time scan avoids an O(n log n) setup cost when the original data lacks a legal binary-search representation.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_then_binary: For one query, preprocessing dominates the logarithmic lookup.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_unsorted: The method is incorrect on arbitrary unsorted data.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.build_and_discard: That still pays unnecessary preprocessing and space.

items.prompt: A sorted copy is built for repeated key lookups, but the output must return the earliest original position for each key. What must the preprocessing preserve?

options.text: Each indexed key must retain enough original-position provenance to answer the earliest-position contract.
Only the sorted key value.
Only the number of records.
The midpoints used during queries.

feedback.reason: Preprocessing is legal only when the indexed representation can answer the complete output contract.
feedback.details: Without provenance, repeated binary lookups may be fast but return the wrong record identity; scanning the original remains correct.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.key_only: Equal keys may have different original positions.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.array_length: Length cannot identify the earliest matching record.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mid_history: Search traces do not replace source identity.

items.prompt: The dataset changes after almost every query. A sorted copy would need to be rebuilt from scratch after each update. Which factor moves the decision boundary back toward scanning?

options.text: The repeated preprocessing or maintenance cost can exceed the saved query work.
The word sorted still makes binary search mandatory.
Queries return booleans.
Midpoint arithmetic remains O(1).

feedback.reason: A preprocessing strategy must include update frequency and index maintenance.
feedback.details: Static-query analysis cannot be transferred unchanged to a highly dynamic workload.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorted_keyword: A stale or repeatedly rebuilt representation changes the total workload.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.query_boolean: Return type does not determine strategy.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.mid_constant: The dominant cost may be rebuilding the representation.

items.prompt: A stable sorted random-access array receives repeated exact queries. No preprocessing is needed. Which comparison is appropriate?

options.text: Use correct binary search for O(log n) per query rather than O(n) scan per query.
Scan because preprocessing cost is zero for both.
Sort before every query to guarantee order.
Use any function called binarySearch without checking its boundaries.

feedback.reason: When the legal ordered representation already exists, repeated queries expose the direct O(log n) versus O(n) contrast.
feedback.details: The conclusion still assumes random access and a query aligned with the sort key.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_because_no_setup: The existing order already supplies the binary-search invariant.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_again: The data is already stable and sorted.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.binary_by_name_only: The implementation still must be correct.

items.prompt: A sorted linked list receives many exact queries. What preprocessing can change the cost boundary most directly?

options.text: Materialize a stable random-access array once, then binary-search it for later queries.
Repeatedly locate linked-list midpoints by traversal and call that O(log n).
Rename nodes to left, right, and mid.
Scan and sort the linked list for every query.

feedback.reason: Preprocessing can change the data-access model as well as its order.
feedback.details: The one-time O(n) materialization and O(n) memory may be worthwhile for enough repeated queries.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.rename_nodes: Names do not provide random access.

items.prompt: Memory is tightly limited and a sorted copy would require O(n) additional storage. The original unsorted data receives a moderate number of queries. What should the decision include?

options.text: Compare repeated scan time with both preprocessing time and the O(n) memory requirement; binary search is not automatically preferable.
Choose binary search because space never affects strategy selection.
Sort the original in place even if original order is part of the contract.
Treat every scan as O(1) because no extra memory is used.

feedback.reason: Repeated-query optimization is constrained by legal mutation and memory budget.
feedback.details: A scan can remain the viable strategy even when its time is higher asymptotically.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.ignore_memory: The sorted copy may violate the memory constraint.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.scan_constant_time: Space and time are distinct dimensions.

items.prompt: A preprocessed sorted index supports fast lookups, but queries sometimes use a different field than the indexed sort key. Which strategy applies to those unmatched queries?

options.text: Scan for that field or build and maintain another query-aligned index if workload justifies it.
Binary-search the existing index because any sorted order is sufficient.
Check the other field only at mid and discard a side.
Return a record matching the indexed field instead.

feedback.reason: Preprocessing is query-specific.
feedback.details: An index transfers only to predicates aligned with its order and preserved output semantics.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.reuse_any_index: The query predicate is not monotonic in the unrelated key order.

items.prompt: Which factors determine whether sorting once and binary-searching repeated queries is worthwhile? Select all that apply.

options.text: The number of queries q.
How often updates invalidate or require maintaining the sorted representation.
Whether reordering or the index preserves the full output contract.
Whether the query helper is called search.

feedback.reason: Preprocessing decisions are workload and contract decisions.
feedback.details: The per-query asymptotic class is only one term in the comparison.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.function_name: Naming has no cost or correctness effect.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.query_count: More queries increase repeated-scan cost and amortize setup.

items.prompt: Which costs belong in the total workload for a sorted-copy binary-search strategy? Select all that apply.

options.text: Copying the original data when mutation is not allowed.
Sorting or otherwise constructing the ordered index.
The sum of all q binary-search query costs.
No cost for updates that rebuild the index.

feedback.reason: The total cost is preprocessing plus queries plus any maintenance.
feedback.details: Leaving out setup or rebuild work systematically biases the comparison toward binary search.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.ignore_updates: Maintenance must be included when updates occur.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.copy_cost: The representation must be created somewhere.

items.prompt: Which workloads favor repeated linear scans? Select all that apply.

options.text: Very few queries on unsorted data with no existing index.
Updates force a full index rebuild before most queries.
The output contract depends on original order and no provenance-preserving index is available.
Many aligned queries on a stable sorted random-access array.

feedback.reason: Scanning can win through lower setup cost, lower memory, or stronger semantic fit.
feedback.details: Repeated binary search wins when order and access already exist or can be amortized safely.

items.prompt: Which changes can move a workload from scan-preferred to binary-search-preferred? Select all that apply.

options.text: Increase the number of aligned queries while keeping data stable.
Receive the data already sorted in random-access form.
Maintain an index incrementally at acceptable update cost.
Change the query to an arbitrary nonmonotonic condition.

feedback.reason: The decision boundary responds to query volume, representation, and maintenance.
feedback.details: Changing to a nonmonotonic predicate moves in the opposite direction.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.increase_q: The one-time setup is spread across more queries.

items.prompt: Arrange the analysis of repeated-query preprocessing.

elements.text: State n, query count q, update frequency, and required outputs.
Describe repeated scan and each legal preprocessed representation.
Derive preprocessing, maintenance, per-query, memory, and output costs.
Choose the strategy for the actual workload and identify when the choice would flip.

feedback.reason: Repeated-query decisions require a complete workload model.
feedback.details: A per-query comparison omits the terms that usually determine the break-even point.

items.prompt: Arrange the lifecycle of a sorted-copy strategy for stable data.

elements.text: Copy the data if original order or immutability must be preserved.
Retain the representation for future queries instead of rebuilding it.

feedback.reason: The representation must exist and persist before repeated queries can amortize it.
feedback.details: Rebuilding per query destroys the intended cost model.

items.prompt: Arrange the review after an update to preprocessed search data.

elements.text: Determine whether the update preserves the index ordering and metadata.
Apply the required incremental repair or full rebuild.
Verify queries will not read stale structure.
Use binary search only after the ordered representation is valid again.

feedback.reason: A stale index invalidates correctness, not just performance.
feedback.details: The maintenance cost belongs to the repeated-query comparison.

items.prompt: An unsorted static array of n values receives q exact queries. Compare q linear scans with sorting once and then binary-searching all queries.
constraints: Comparison sorting costs O(n log n).
Each binary search costs O(log n).

preprocessed_time: O(n log n + q log n)
O(n log n + q log n)

preprocessed_time.sort_plus_queries: O(n log n + q log n)

feedback.reason: The workload comparison includes both setup and every query.
feedback.details: The preprocessed strategy gains as q grows, but it is not automatically superior for small q.

items.prompt: Using the same model, approximately which symbolic query scale makes sorting once competitive with repeated scans for large n?
constraints: Compare qn with n log n + q log n.
Treat n - log n as Θ(n) for large n.

feedback.reason: The setup cost becomes amortized after roughly logarithmically many full-array scans.
feedback.details: The exact practical crossover depends on constants, but the symbolic comparison shows why query count matters.

items.prompt: A sorted copy of n records is built and stores each record plus its original index. q binary searches are then performed. Select total time and additional representation space.

time: O(n log n + q log n)
O(n log n + q log n)

time.copy_sort_queries: O(n log n + q log n)

feedback.reason: Preserving original identity can make preprocessing correct but consumes linear extra memory.
feedback.details: The time-space tradeoff must be checked against the workload constraints.

items.prompt: A dataset receives u updates. After each update, a full O(n log n) sorted index rebuild occurs, followed by r binary-search queries. Compare this with scanning each of the ur queries without an index.
constraints: n is treated as the same scale for each update epoch.

rebuild_strategy_time: O(u n log n + u r log n)
O(n log n + r log n)
O(u n log n + u r log n)

rebuild_strategy_time.per_epoch_rebuild_and_queries: O(u n log n + u r log n)

feedback.reason: Update frequency creates a new break-even comparison per epoch.
feedback.details: A large total q is not enough if every small batch of queries pays another full rebuild.

items.prompt: A sorted linked list is copied once into an array in O(n), then q exact queries use O(log n) binary search. Compare with q linked-list scans.
constraints: The copied array uses O(n) additional space.

feedback.reason: Preprocessing can create random access as well as preserve order.
feedback.details: The benefit must justify the linear representation space.

items.prompt: Each key comparison costs C. A stable sorted array receives q exact queries. Compare q binary searches with q linear scans.
constraints: Binary search performs O(log n) comparisons per query.
A scan performs O(n) comparisons per query in the worst case.

feedback.reason: Expensive comparisons multiply both strategies, while binary search reduces how many comparisons are needed.
feedback.details: The legality assumptions still precede this cost advantage.

items.prompt: A maintained ordered index supports O(log n) query and O(n) update. A plain array supports O(n) query and O(1) append. For q queries and u appends, compare total workload time.
constraints: The O(n) index update includes maintaining sorted order.

feedback.reason: The best representation depends jointly on query and update volume.
feedback.details: An index with cheap queries can lose when updates are frequent and expensive.

contrastSets.transferBoundary: Pay preprocessing cost only when the query volume, mutation model, memory budget, and preserved semantics make the total workload cheaper than repeated scans.
